question: Diode lasers are the workhorse of AMO labs. An extended-cavity diode laser (ECDL) in Littrow configuration uses a diffraction grating to narrow the linewidth and tune the wavelength. The key components and their function:

options: Diode chip (gain medium, $\sim 1{-}5$ mW single-mode power); diffraction grating (first-order reflection feeds back to diode, zeroth order is output beam); piezoelectric transducer (PZT) on grating (fine wavelength tuning by tilting grating); temperature controller (coarse wavelength tuning, $\sim 0.3$ nm/°C for GaAs diodes)
The ECDL uses a Fabry-Pérot etalon instead of a diffraction grating
The grating zeroth order provides feedback and the first order is the output
Temperature tuning is faster than PZT tuning in an ECDL
passive.explanation: ECDL linewidth: feedback from the grating extends the effective cavity length from $\sim 0.3$ mm (diode chip) to $\sim 5$ cm (chip + free space), reducing linewidth from $\sim 100$ MHz (free-running) to $\sim 100$ kHz–1 MHz. Current tuning: $\sim 300$ MHz/mA (fast, $< 1\,\mu$s). PZT tuning: $\sim 300$ MHz/V, bandwidth $\sim 10$ kHz. Temperature tuning: $\sim 10$ GHz/°C (slow, minutes). Mode-hop-free tuning range: typically 10–20 GHz. The Littman-Metcalf configuration uses the grating at grazing incidence — narrower linewidth but lower output power.

active.prompt: Match each ECDL parameter to its typical value and physical origin:

pairs.definition: $\sim 100$ MHz (Schawlow-Townes + $1/f$ noise, carrier fluctuations); white frequency noise floor
$\sim 100$ kHz–1 MHz; reduced by external cavity feedback; still limited by acoustic/thermal noise
$< 1$ kHz–10 Hz; limited by vibration of reference cavity; state-of-art: $< 1$ Hz on ultra-stable cavities
$\sim 10{-}20$ GHz — limited by competition between external cavity modes; determined by PZT stroke and current ramp synchronisation

active.explanation: Mode-hop-free tuning: simultaneously ramp PZT (cavity length) and injection current (gain peak). The PZT changes mode spacing and the current shifts the gain curve — when synchronised, the laser slides through frequencies without jumping to a different longitudinal mode. Commercial ECDLs (Toptica DL pro, MOGLabs CEL): typically $> 10$ GHz mode-hop-free range, sub-MHz linewidth.

question: The Pound-Drever-Hall (PDH) technique is the standard method for locking a laser frequency to a high-finesse reference cavity. The error signal is generated by:

options: Phase modulating the laser with an EOM at frequency $\Omega_m$; the reflected field from the cavity produces a beat between the carrier and sidebands; this beat is demodulated at $\Omega_m$ to produce an antisymmetric error signal proportional to laser frequency offset from cavity resonance
Measuring the transmitted power through the cavity and feeding it directly to the laser current
Detecting the cavity buildup with a photodiode inside the cavity mirror
Beating the laser against a second reference laser to generate the error signal
passive.explanation:
PDH error signal near resonance: $\varepsilon \approx -8\sqrt{P_c P_s}/(P_{m inc}) \cdot (\delta
u/\Delta
u_{m fsr}) \cdot \mathcal{F}/\pi$, where $P_c$ is carrier power, $P_s$ sideband power. Discriminant slope: $D = dV_{m error}/d
u \approx 8\eta\sqrt{P_c P_s}/\Omega_m$ V/Hz. Bandwidth: the PDH signal has a bandwidth of $\sim\Delta
u_{m cav}$ (cavity linewidth, typically $\sim$ MHz), much wider than the traditional side-of-fringe technique ($\sim \Gamma_{m laser}$). This wide bandwidth enables fast servo corrections. Used to lock lasers to $< 1$ Hz linewidth on ultra-stable (ULE glass) cavities.

question: Optical injection locking transfers the frequency and phase stability of a 'master' laser to a higher-power 'slave' laser. The injection locking range (bandwidth) scales as:

options: $\Delta\nu_{\rm lock} \propto (P_{\rm master}/P_{\rm slave})^{1/2}$ — injecting a small fraction of master power into the slave cavity pulls the slave's oscillation frequency to the master's; within the locking range, the slave is phase-coherent with the master
The locking range is independent of the injected power
Injection locking requires the master and slave to be at exactly the same wavelength
The slave laser must have a higher finesse cavity than the master
passive.explanation: Locking range (Adler equation): $\Delta\omega_{\rm lock} = \omega_0/(2Q_L)\sqrt{P_{\rm inj}/P_{\rm slave}}$ where $Q_L$ is the slave laser $Q$. For a diode laser slave ($Q_L \sim 10^8$, $P_{\rm inj}/P_{\rm slave} \sim 10^{-4}$): $\Delta\nu \sim 100$ MHz — sufficient to pull a free-running slave (linewidth $\sim 10$ MHz) within the master's range. Benefits: slave output power up to 1 W (tapered amplifiers) with master coherence. Disadvantages: must monitor locking (output spectrum or heterodyne beat with master).

question: Saturated absorption spectroscopy is the most common laboratory technique for laser frequency stabilisation to an atomic reference. The setup requires:

options: Counter-propagating pump (strong, chopped) and probe (weak) beams through an atomic vapour cell at room temperature; the probe transmission shows Doppler-broadened absorption with narrow Lamb dips at the true atomic transition frequencies — used as absolute frequency references for locking
A room-temperature cell with the laser at 45° to the atomic beam
Two identical lasers, one of which serves as a frequency reference
Cooling the vapour cell to liquid nitrogen temperature to reduce Doppler broadening
passive.explanation: Practical setup: Rb cell (no temperature stabilisation needed — room temperature gives enough vapour pressure from isotope with $\sim 1\%$ natural abundance for $^{87}$Rb). Pump beam retroreflected or counter-propagating. Modulation: chop the pump at $\sim 10$ kHz; lock-in detect probe signal. The Lamb dips at transitions and crossover resonances provide $\sim 10{-}30$ MHz wide features. Typical lock bandwidth: $\sim 100$ kHz with a PI controller. Frequency stability: $\sim 100$ kHz long-term (determined by pressure shifts, magnetic fields, temperature). Better: modulation transfer spectroscopy (MTS) or frequency modulation spectroscopy (FMS) — give narrower, dispersive error signals.

question: A tapered amplifier (TA) chip is a semiconductor optical amplifier with a tapered geometry that provides high output power (0.5–2 W) while maintaining the spatial mode quality of the injected seed. Key parameters and practical considerations:

options: TA output power 0.5–2 W at 780 nm (Rb) or 852 nm (Cs) from $\sim 10{-}50$ mW seed; amplified spontaneous emission (ASE) pedestal $\sim 10^{-3}$ of total power; output beam astigmatic and elliptical requiring cylindrical lens correction; sensitive to back-reflection (isolator mandatory)
TAs operate without any seed laser — they produce a fully coherent output spontaneously
TA chips are insensitive to back-reflection and do not require optical isolators
TA output is a perfect circular Gaussian beam directly from the chip facet
passive.explanation: TA practical tips: (1) Always maintain seed injection before applying drive current — the TA will lase on ASE without seed and may damage itself or the experiment. (2) Optical isolator (60 dB) between TA output and experiment is mandatory — any reflection into the TA causes instability and catastrophic failure. (3) TA chip lifetime: $\sim 5000$ h at rated current; degrade faster at higher current or temperature. (4) ASE filtering: bandpass filter or reflection from a diffraction grating can reduce ASE from $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ of total power. (5) Mode matching: use anamorphic prism pair + spherical lens to circularise the elliptical output ($\sim 4:1$ aspect ratio).
